---
pagetitle: "Harness Engineering"
title: "[Harness Engineering]{.amber}"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-color: "#2C3E50"
  data-background-image: figs/banner.png
  data-background-size: 24%
  data-background-position: right 20% center
  class: dark-centered
  style: text-align:left;
---

## Why a third part exists {.light-centered .center}

Parts 1 & 2 cover **short-lived tasks** well.

For tasks that take **hours or days**, single-agent prompting breaks down:

- The agent praises its own mediocre output
- Context fills up → performance degrades
- Agent declares victory while features are stubbed
- You spend more time correcting than building

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%;"}
A **harness** is the structured environment that keeps long-running work reliable.
:::

## The through-line {.dark-centered .center}

> *"Every component in a harness encodes an assumption about what the model can't do on its own — and those assumptions are worth stress testing."*
>
> — Anthropic Engineering, *Harness Design for Long-Running Application Development* (Mar 2026)

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 70%;"}
Hold onto this. It's the whole part in one sentence.
:::

## Today {.light-centered .center}

Three sections, ~25 minutes:

1. **[Components]{.teal}** — what's inside a harness
2. **[Practices]{.amber}** — what successful teams do
3. **[Design Your Own]{.coral}** — a staged path from baseline to full harness

## [Section 1]{.amber}<br>Components of a Harness {.dark-centered .center}

## Definition {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 90%;"}
A harness is an orchestrated system of **specialized agents and tools** that guide Claude through complex multi-step tasks. Rather than one model doing everything, work is decomposed into phases with **different agent personas**, each handling what the base model struggles with alone.
:::

<br>

Three layers:

1. **Agent specialization** — different jobs, different prompts, different contexts
2. **Infrastructure** — communication, handoffs, tools
3. **Verification systems** — how you check that work is actually done

## Layer 1: Agent specialization {.light-centered .center}

The durable triad for long-running coding work:

| Agent | Job | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|
| **[Planner]{.teal}** | Expand prompt → detailed spec | Base model under-specifies when implementing |
| **[Generator]{.amber}** | Do the actual work | Focused execution, no judgment burden |
| **[Evaluator]{.coral}** | Independently grade output | Generators **inflate scores** when grading themselves |

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%;"}
*"Agents reliably skew positive when grading their own work."*
:::

## Layer 2: Infrastructure {.light-centered .center}

The plumbing that lets multi-agent work survive long sessions:

- **File-based communication** — one writes, another reads. Files survive resets.
- **Structured artifact handoffs** — `SPEC.md`, `IMPLEMENTATION.md`, `REVIEW.md`
- **Version control** — every checkpoint is a commit. Rewindable.
- **Sprint contracts** — generator and evaluator negotiate "done" **before** work begins
- **Interactive testing tools** — Playwright MCP, HTTP MCP. Use the output, don't just read it.

## Layer 3: Verification systems {.light-centered .center}

Most people get this layer wrong.

- **Concrete, gradable criteria** — not *"is this good?"* but *"does this satisfy criteria 1, 2, 3 at score N?"*
- **Few-shot calibration** — example outputs with detailed score breakdowns
- **Hard thresholds** — sprint fails below threshold. No "directionally good enough."
- **Interactive (not static)** — let the evaluator *use* the output. Click, type, navigate, hit the API.

<br>

> Subjective questions get subjective answers. Gradable criteria get gradable ones.

## A complete harness {.light-centered .center}

```text
       prompt
         │
         ▼
   ┌──────────┐
   │  PLANNER │── writes SPEC.md ──┐
   └──────────┘                    │
                                   ▼
                            sprint contract
                                   │
                                   ▼
   ┌────────────┐  reads spec  ┌──────────┐
   │ GENERATOR  │◀────────────▶│ EVALUATOR│
   │ writes code│  writes      │ tests    │
   │            │  REVIEW.md   │ outputs  │
   └────────────┘              └──────────┘
         │                          │
         └──── git commits ─────────┘
                    │
                    ▼
              (next sprint or done)
```

## [Section 2]{.amber}<br>Successful Practices {.dark-centered .center}

## Practice 1 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%; font-size: 1.3em;"}
[Separate]{.teal} generation [from]{.amber} evaluation.
:::

<br>

- Evaluator is a **separate agent** with its own context
- Tune it to be **skeptical**, not lenient
- File-based handoff: generator exits, evaluator reads fresh
- Single biggest quality lever

> Do not ask Claude to evaluate output Claude just produced.

## Practice 2 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%; font-size: 1.3em;"}
Turn [subjective]{.teal} judgments into [gradable]{.amber} criteria.
:::

<br>

Frontend design example:

| Criterion | Weight | What it means |
|---|---|---|
| Design quality | 25% | Layout, hierarchy, contrast |
| Originality | 20% | Not a generic template |
| Craft | 25% | Spacing, typography, micro-interactions |
| Functionality | 30% | Features actually work end-to-end |

<br>

> Calibrate with **few-shot examples**: 2–3 outputs with annotated scores.

## Practice 3 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%; font-size: 1.3em;"}
Use context [resets]{.teal}, not [compaction]{.coral}.
:::

<br>

- In-place compaction creates **"context anxiety"** — model senses fill, wraps up early
- Resets clear context, model reads `SPEC.md` + `STATE.md` fresh
- This is **why** the harness is built around files

<br>

When to reset:

- Between sprints
- When the model starts cutting corners
- When you've corrected the same mistake twice

## Practice 4 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%; font-size: 1.3em;"}
[Decompose]{.amber} complex tasks.
:::

<br>

A multi-hour task done in one shot **fails**.<br>The same task split into eight one-hour sprints **succeeds**.

<br>

Each sprint should have:

- A **single clear deliverable**
- **Success criteria known in advance** (the sprint contract)
- A scope that fits in one context window
- File handoffs and a reset between sprints

## Practice 5 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%; font-size: 1.3em;"}
[Iterate]{.coral} the harness as the model improves.
:::

<br>

When a new Claude releases:

- Is the planner still load-bearing?
- Does the evaluator still catch what it caught last quarter?
- Are sprint contracts still needed?

<br>

> *"As models continue to improve, the space of interesting harness combinations doesn't shrink. It moves."*

## Failure modes & their fixes {.light-centered .center}

| Failure | Fix |
|---|---|
| **Self-evaluation bias** | Separate evaluator agent (P1) |
| **Context degradation** | Reset + file handoffs (P3) |
| **Specification brittleness** | Keep spec high-level, use sprint contracts |
| **Incomplete QA testing** | Prompt evaluator to probe deeply, refine iteratively |
| **Feature stubbing** | Evaluator tests for completeness, files bugs |

## [Section 3]{.amber}<br>Design Your Own Harness {.dark-centered .center}

## Start simple {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%; font-size: 1.2em;"}
*"Find the simplest solution possible.<br>Only increase complexity when needed."*
:::

<br>

A staged path follows. **Stop at the earliest stage that works.**<br>Most workflows never need to leave Stage 0.

## Stage 0 — single-agent baseline {.light-centered .center}

Just Claude Code, in your project directory.

- A `CLAUDE.md`
- Maybe a few skills (Part 2)

<br>

You **don't need a harness yet**. Signals you've outgrown it:

- Tasks regularly span multiple sessions
- Model declares victory on incomplete work
- More time correcting than building
- Quality varies wildly between attempts

## Stage 1 — add a verifier {.light-centered .center}

The cheapest harness upgrade:

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%;"}
A second Claude Code session with a "skeptical reviewer" prompt.
:::

<br>

| Session A (Writer) | Session B (Reviewer) |
|---|---|
| Implement feature X | (waits) |
| (writes code, commits) | Review `@src/...`. Edge cases, races, consistency. |
| Address the review feedback | (waits) |

<br>

> You haven't built a "harness" yet. You've decoupled generation from evaluation. **That's most of the win.**

## Stage 2 — add a planner {.light-centered .center}

When scope keeps drifting:

- Tasks start without enough scope definition
- Implementation doesn't match what you wanted
- First draft solves a different problem

<br>

Implementation:

- A planning session produces `SPEC.md`
- A fresh implementation session reads it

<br>

> Same shape as Claude Code's built-in **plan mode**, scaled across sessions.

## Stage 3 — sprint contracts {.light-centered .center}

```markdown
# Sprint 3 Contract

## Goal
Add OAuth login flow to the existing auth module.

## Done means
- [ ] User can complete the Google sign-in flow
- [ ] Session persists across page reloads
- [ ] Existing email/password login still works
- [ ] All new code has unit tests, all tests pass
- [ ] No TODOs or stubs in shipped code

## Out of scope
- GitHub OAuth (separate sprint)
- UI redesign of the login page
```

<br>

> Disagreements happen during **drafting**, not at the end.

## Stage 4 — interactive verification {.light-centered .center}

When static review stops catching real issues, give the evaluator tools to **use** the output:

- **Frontend** → Playwright MCP (click, type, navigate, screenshot)
- **Backend** → HTTP MCP (hit the API, assert on responses)
- **Data** → query MCP (run the analysis end-to-end)

<br>

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Static review catches

- Typos
- Structure issues
- Surface bugs
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Interactive review catches

- Race conditions
- Integration failures
- Real edge cases
:::
:::

## Stage 5 — define gradable criteria {.light-centered .center}

Write down **3–6 criteria** for "good" output in your domain.

<br>

For each criterion:

- A **rubric** (what counts as 1, 3, 5)
- 2–3 **few-shot examples** with detailed score breakdowns
- A **hard threshold** below which the sprint fails

<br>

> Weight criteria by where the model is naturally weak.<br>Feed the rubric into the evaluator's system prompt.

## Stage 6 — stress-test on every release {.light-centered .center}

When a new Claude releases:

1. Run an existing task through the harness once
2. For each component, ask: *"Is this still load-bearing?"*
3. Disable load-bearing components one at a time. Re-run.
4. Anything you can disable without quality dropping → **delete it**
5. With the freed budget, add components that push further

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%;"}
Otherwise harnesses accumulate cruft and slow you down.
:::

## A starter checklist {.light-centered .center}

For your next non-trivial project:

- [ ] What's my baseline failure mode?
- [ ] Do I need a planner, an evaluator, or both?
- [ ] What are my 3–6 gradable success criteria?
- [ ] What does my sprint contract template look like?
- [ ] What tool does my evaluator use to interactively verify?
- [ ] Where do my structured handoffs live?
- [ ] What's my reset trigger?

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 70%;"}
Answer these → you have a harness.
:::

## Workshop wrap {.dark-centered .center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
### [Part 1]{.teal}

Vocabulary.<br><br>Assistants vs agents.<br>Four components.<br>Handy tools.
:::

::: {.column width="34%"}
### [Part 2]{.amber}

Behaviors layer.<br><br>Skill design.<br>Gotchas section.<br>Symlinks for sharing.
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### [Part 3]{.coral}

Operating model.<br><br>Planner / Generator / Evaluator.<br>Sprint contracts.<br>Interactive verification.
:::
:::

## The through-line, again {.dark-centered .center}

> *"Every component in your setup — every CLAUDE.md instruction, every skill, every harness piece — encodes an assumption about what the model can't do on its own.<br>Treat those assumptions as living. Stress-test them as the model improves."*

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%; font-size: 1.3em;"}
Stop handcrafting. Start conducting.
:::

<br>

Thank you.
